I’m honestly blown away by what you’ve built with Minos. The architecture around blind benchmarking, validator-side execution, and the continuous 72-minute challenge loop is one of the most elegant scientific uses of Bittensor I’ve seen so far. The way HelixForge injects mutations directly at the read level and separates miner configuration from execution is particularly impressive. Really well done.

I had one technical question I’m genuinely curious about:

How do you validate that HelixForge’s synthetic mutations remain statistically indistinguishable from real biological variants across different genomic contexts (coverage bias, repeat regions, platform-specific sequencing noise, etc.), so that miners can’t accidentally overfit to artifacts of the synthetic mutation generator rather than improving real-world variant calling accuracy?

In other words, what methodology do you use to ensure the benchmark distribution stays aligned with real human genomic variation over time?

sounds like a bot.
subnet doesn't even have any repo, and you speak as if they are already sitting on the Nobel Prize.

Thanks. We’re glad to have the opportunity to build this properly without rushing. Public repo is coming soon.

I'll probably getting a bit technical here but based on your questions I'm assuming that's OK: HelixForge’s injection engine is core IP, so I can’t share the exact mechanics. That said, once we’ve generated enough data over the next few months via miners/validators, we plan to submit the full pipeline including HelixForge step to a peer-reviewed journal. So the methodology will be documented there in more detail.

Without going into too much detail: we've built this based on my prior experience building/validating variant-calling pipelines. The key reason the mutations stay biologically realistic is that we operate directly on sequencing reads, not just VCFs which are prone to easy manipulation. We’re modifying real reads in their real local context, so the injected signal inherits coverage, GC/mappability effects, strand bias, and other locus-specific noise. I think an important distinction is we're not generating random synthetic mutations de novo, we’re editing within real data and sequencing context, which helps keep things like Ti/Tv, het/hom balance, and Mendelian expectations consistent. We’ll also expand into harder regions over time (segdups, TRs , HLA, etc.) as the subnet matures.

On platform noise: the subnet is Illumina short-read only for now, mostly because that’s still the workhorse everywhere and the error models are well understood. Long-read is on the roadmap as they mature in the coming years. Ultima/Element look promising, but there isn’t enough reference data yet for a rigorous benchmark with them, and we’re not targeting Ion Torrent for obvious reasons.

Follow us on X for more details.

It’ll go live soon. Since we want to bring the broader scientific community in early and share our results with them, we need to be careful with the small implementation and reproducibility details. We’d rather start on solid footing than rush a first release, especially in genomics where the little details can end up mattering a lot.

Hi @Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ]

What do you think about this @Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ] ?

All good points - and I appreciate the feedback. We’ve tested this extensively on our local testnet. If your machine meets the minimum specs listed in the repo, you should be able to complete a mining round with time to spare. The most time consuming and resource intensive tool is DeepVariant, and you can run that in around 30mins for the specified genome size. 
For validators, a larger machine is needed, but even then scoring is still comfortably within range. A validator can score one miner roughly every 5–6 minutes. So while the timing is somewhat tight for sure, we want to keep the pace high and we believe it’s achievable.
Of course, live subnet conditions may differ significantly from local testing. That’s exactly why we’ve been putting so much effort in over the past couple of weeks to make sure we’re ready to adapt quickly once we go live. We're basically preparing the team so that when / if things breakdown and we need quick adjustments, we are ready to do so.
5 * 50 = 250 mins(3x current round time)
@Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ] The region is changed per round? How about the number of mutations? Do you keep the coverage at the same level?

Each validator scores a subset of miners per round, with overlap between validators for cross-validation. By running rounds every 72 minutes, we accumulate a lot more data points over time rather than doing longer rounds. The law of large numbers ensures every miner's performance converges to its true value as observations grow. Yuma consensus then aggregates weights across all validators, so even though each has a partial view, the network-level ranking stabilizes quickly with frequent rounds.
New miners must also score in at least 5 rounds before they're eligible. This warmup period means their performance gets evaluated by multiple validators across multiple rounds, and by the time they're eligible, their EMA is starting to stabilize.
Also worth noting that the 5-6 minutes per miner I mentioned is a conservative estimate on a modest CPU machine (we have provided the very minimal compute requirements for both miners and validators in the repo). For example, the full validator cycle on one of Targon's large CPU instances, completes in under 100 seconds per miner. At that speed, a single validator could score 40+ miners per round. GPU machines would be even faster. That said, we'd still prefer doing subsetting with more frequent rounds because we need fast iterations from miners. And of course, if any of this proves unfeasible in practice, we'll address it quickly.

Yes, the evaluation window changes each round, so miners face a different genomic context every time - but they will not stray too far from the previous round. For example we won’t go from a easy region to suddenly asking you to perform variant calling in the HLA region. Mutation count targets ~100 but the exact count varies slightly due to pre- and post-validation filtering we need to perform to ensure the mutations are biologically sound. Coverage is firmly in the high-coverage regime for this phase, so no low-pass nuances requiring imputation-aware genotyping or anything like that is needed. You can expect 20-30x per round, but that does vary, and it's something you should account for in your configs as coverage directly impacts variant caller sensitivity and specificity.

@Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ] Are there any regulatory hurdles ahead and have you any timelines for these?

This is a great question. The short answer is that regulatory burden is minimal for us by design.
On the benchmarking side, we use entirely synthetic genomes, these are variants that are computationally spiked into reference sequences of publicly available genomes (thousands of them are available with high coverage), so there are no HIPAA, GDPR, or patient data concerns whatsoever.
On the deployment side, our goal is to offer optimized variant callers as an API service, you can think of it as a similar approach to Illumina's DRAGEN. In that model, the clinical labs consuming the API hold the regulatory obligations (CLIA/CAP, IVD), not us. We provide the computational engine, not the clinical decision. 
So basically no regulatory gates are blocking our roadmap, our milestones are engineering/scientific ones, not regulatory ones.

messages.content:
Hey, yes, for Phase 1 we’re using a winner-takes-all model, where the top miner receives all emissions.
That makes sense for Phase 1 because hyperparameter configuration has a relatively low barrier to entry. In later phases, we’ll likely move to a more proportional structure, where the top X miners receive emissions.

Also, we won’t be using raw F1 scores directly for ranking. We’re using a custom scoring framework that incorporates F1 but is designed to reflect real performance more accurately. Raw F1 can sometimes make results look artificially strong, which is useful for publications, but less useful when the goal is to actually improve models.

Thanks for the clarification! Could you share more details about the custom scoring framework? Specifically, what metrics does it incorporate beyond F1, and is there anything miners should optimize for beyond hyperparameter tuning to maximize their score?

Yeah of course. 
The scoring goes beyond just F1 because that barely differentiates real performance improvement. Our scoring is a composite that looks at four things, weighted by importance.

1. The bulk of your score comes from core accuracy across both SNPs and INDELs, but we apply a nonlinear curve that magnifies small differences at the top end. So when everyone's clustered around 0.99, the scoring still meaningfully separates miners.
2. On top of that we look at completeness, meaning are you actually calling variants in the hard regions or just skipping them to protect your precision? We penalize that. 
3. We also look at false positive rate separately, so you can't just lower all your thresholds to catch everything either. 
4. And finally there's a quality check based on known biological expectations of a real human genome (Ti/Tv, Het/Hom ratios for example) that catches systematic biases in your calls to call out fake calls. So if you're using a totally random configuration that would just output garbage you'll get penalized because these are not biologically plausibe (this would happen if you perform worse than the default settings).

The whole point is you can't game one axis without hurting another. The best strategy is genuinely well-calibrated configs that produce balanced, biologically plausible output across both variant types. Think about how your tool handles the precision-recall tradeoff in different contexts rather than just chasing one number if that makes sense.
Hi @Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ], when the emission will be relased?

Thank you for the detailed breakdown! Really helpful to understand the four components. We’ve been optimizing specifically for the precision-recall tradeoff on both SNPs and INDELs, so this confirms we’re on the right track. Looking forward to the first round! 🔥

That's a really solid methodological choice, 
without a credible upper bound on current tools, 
any phase 2 improvement is ambiguous.
Also makes the scoring formula (Core F1 60% / 
Completeness 15% / FP 15% / Quality 10%) feel 
well-calibrated for the goal,rewards the miners 
who genuinely push the tools to their limits 
rather than gaming edge cases.

Yep, and one thing I’m exploring for phase 2 is disease-specific synthetic genomes rather than the current approach with randomly drawn mutations.
I think the idea is to test whether different genetic architectures can be exploited by homebrew pipelines in different ways. For example, if a disorder has a highly polygenic architecture, there may be structure there that a custom caller or post-processing pipeline can use differently than it would for a more Mendelian-style signal.
That’s still early, but I think it could open up a much more interesting search space than just having general-purpose variant calling, but better. Imagine all the possibilities we can have with that.

That's a really sharp framing. Most variant calling 
work treats genomic architecture as a nuisance 
parameter, your approach flips it into the signal 
itself.
Polygenic vs Mendelian has fundamentally different 
statistical properties (effect size distribution, 
LD structure, allelic heterogeneity), so a pipeline 
that "knows what it's looking for" should 
outperform a general one by meaningful margins. 
Really curious where that goes.
For phase 1 I'll focus on exhausting current tools 
as you suggested. Will ping you when mainnet goes 
live, keen to see the first results.

The concern is before that: the team controls a fully blackbox mutation generation and knows the ground truth before miners do. From a miner’s perspective, that creates an unverifiable operator advantage. A team run anon miner could tune against HelixForge’s exact private distribution, or worse, generate BAMs whose injected mutations are especially recoverable by a known config. Validators would still score honestly, and the miner would “legitimately” win, and it would be impossible for ANYONE to distinguish that from fair competition.

Mainly theres been many subnets built on the premise of obfuscating an owner advantage to self mine. For anyone on your team I wld expect the temptation to do so given your built in advantage will be high, and it's less attractive for miners to invest time into solving a problem on a potentially rigged game.

@Centrum | Minos SN107 [ミ] 

hey, wanted to throw out something for discussion — i think the
current 64 slots + winner-takes-all combination might actually
be hurting the subnet more than helping. would extending
emissions to top 10 make sense?

quick concrete example so this isn't abstract:

we registered on may 1, currently rank 7 with EMA 0.67, eligible,
254 evaluations submitted. on every metric of "active contributor",
things look fine.

but here's what taomarketcap shows: we're literally #1 on the
"to be removed" list. avg lifespan on this subnet is 3d 16h.
my UID has been alive 2d 18h — i'm in the dereg risk zone now,
and the next registration knocks me out regardless of EMA.

zooming out — that "3d 16h avg lifespan" is the alarm. with
64 slots and WTA, the math forces this:
  - top 1-3 earn emissions
  - the other 60+ burn TAO every cycle and exit before recouping
  - burn → dereg → re-register → repeat
the subnet ends up running on a treadmill of ~60 miners losing
money to subsidize the top few. for diversity of training data
and product resilience, that's the worst possible structure.

reasons extending to top 10 would help:

1) product continuity. with WTA, only #1 has real skin in the
   game. if they leave, subnet quality drops overnight. top 5-10
   gives you a deep bench: #2 naturally steps up, product stays
   consistent. arguably the strongest argument — your subnet's
   only as resilient as the layer below the leader.

2) training data diversity. WTA pushes everyone to copy the
   leader, so over time you get 64 instances of one config.
   spreading rewards keeps strategies diverse, which is exactly
   what you said you want for the RL training data.

3) sustainable miner economy. right now ~60 of 64 miners are
   structurally guaranteed to lose money on each lifespan. that's
   why avg lifespan is 3d 16h — it's literally the time it takes
   for unprofitable miners to give up. top 10 with even small
   distribution lets serious contributors break even and stick
   around long enough to actually optimize (which = better
   training data for you).

4) trust signal. wider, transparent distribution mitigates the
   self-mining concentration concern. with WTA, one well-placed
   hotkey captures everything; with top 10, even a colluding
   group needs to maintain quality across multiple hotkeys.

rough split idea — top 3 still gets the bulk (~57-60%), ranks
4-10 share ~30-35%, rest burned or to treasury. keeps the sharp
incentive on top while broadening the floor.

Thanks for the thoughtful message, and I appreciate you being involved in Minos from the start.

You are raising fair points. Not sharing the correct answers is intentional though. I thought a lot about this since this is something that others have also raised last week. In this first phase, we are trying to see how far miners can improve variant-calling performance under blind evaluation, without access to the truth data.

That matters because sharing the truth changes the question. We would no longer be measuring how well miners can generalize to hidden truth. We would be measuring how well they can adapt to a known benchmark. Both are useful, but they are different experiments.

There are a few possible fixes, but each has tradeoffs:

We could spread emissions beyond winner-takes-all, but then a top miner could run multiple similar miners and capture more emissions without adding much new value.

We could share outputs after each round, but that would weaken the blind baseline we are trying to establish as our hypothesis.

We could share the top config after each round, but then the competition becomes less about independent exploration and more about slightly beating the current best config.

This is part of why scientific benchmarking is hard, especially in decentralized systems. You need enough transparency for participants to understand the process, but enough hidden information to prevent overfitting to the benchmark.

For now, we want to let the current setup run for about a week. Last week was mostly about fixing bugs and getting validators in sync. Now that the system is working, we need clean baseline data before making major changes.

The competition is not over. We are still very early, and feedback like this helps us decide how Minos should evolve. Once again, I really appreciate your thoughtful message.

Following up on this, another possible way to approach this is moving from EMA-based winners to per-round winners.

Over the past few days since validators synced up, we’ve seen that once a miner gets near the top with a high EMA, they can stay there and keep collecting emissions, even without winning every round or improving on their work.

A per-round winner model would reward the best miner in each round directly, rather than having them build up their EMA over sucessive rounds. This would make the top spot change more often and better incentivize miners to keep improving their configs every round instead of pushing EMA up and coasting.

We’re currently running backward and forward simulations to see how removing EMA and replacing it with per-round winners using advanced scorer would affect performance, win-rate distribution, and emissions behavior.

Thanks everyone for your patience. Here’s what happened:

After our initial bootstrapping during launch week, the network had been running smoothly. As we prepared to ramp up and reduce subnet bootstrapping, we moved our validator to a more long-term machine over the weekend.

During the migration, the validator was restarted and tried to recover its previous state. That recovery request temporarily hit a platform rate limit that silently failed. As a result, the validator temporarily saw 0 eligible miners for weight setting, even though miner scores did exist. Once we restarted the validator, state recovery succeeded and validation went back to normal.

We are monitoring the next rounds closely and will add safeguards so a failed startup recovery cannot silently produce an all-burn weight submission again.

hey, 
I’d think about the miner task less as recovering an answer key from truth files, and more as a statistical optimization problem.

In variant calling, you don’t get ground truth at inference time. You get a BAM, a reference, and noisy read evidence. The caller (be it dv, gatk, or bcftools) has to decide when the evidence is strong enough to call a real variant while controlling false positives.

We want to preserve that real world setup. Validators need hidden truth so the network can score objectively and reward miners. If miners had the answer key, the task would stop being variant-calling optimization and become fitting to known labels, even if it is from previous rounds.

The intended workflow to improve performance is to save prior BAMs, track submitted configs, compare scores over time, and **run controlled experiments across many rounds**. This is also why we added dust weight for the top 10 miners, so strong miners and those who are attempting to improve their performance, are protected from deregistration and can keep iterating instead of being forced out while optimizing.
We can already see from the leaderboard that systematic iteration is possible. Some UIDs are consistently near the top, which shows they are improving over repeated rounds rather than relying on one lucky config. Sometimes you see them fall off and they get back again.

So the goal is not to get the truth file and optimize on that. The goal is to build a generalizable variant-calling strategy that performs well on unseen BAMs under hidden truth, which is much closer to the real scientific and clinical setting. In the real world scenario, you do not get access to truth sets because truth sets are static and unique to each individual. that's why we are generating a new bam for each round.

We already expose all of this. For per-round detail: 
GET /scoring/rounds/{round_id}/scores returns SNP/INDEL TP/snp_metrics and indel_metricstv, het/hom, region_fp counts, runtime plus region and chromosome on the round payload.

Yo @emphy[τ, τ] 

on truth set / background variants:

Current scoring is focused on injected synthetic mutations, we are not on rewarding recovery of the sample’s background germline variants. 

So if your caller emits a real germline variant that is not part of the injected mutation set, it does not count as a true positive for the main score, but if you are doing the work correctly, you should get those variants as well. 
The main TP/FN/F1 metrics are recomputed against the synthetic mutations truth set.

Also, there is an overcalling guardrail across the challenge region. If your config floods the region with lots of extra calls to try to hit the injected synthetic mutations by brute force, those extra unsupported calls can become false positives and hurt your score. FDA / hap.py does not include this because they assume honest participation from each lab, but we had to incorporate this guardrail to avoid gaming. So basically the miner needs to recover the injected variants accurately without excessive regional overcalling.  hap.py / vcfeval codebase is fully public, so the scoring path is transparent and auditable.

 on determinism:

For a fixed (config, round), scoring is always intended to be deterministic.

The round BAM is already generated and downsampled before it is shared, so there is no new random downsampling during validator scoring. The validator reruns the submitted config against the same BAM/reference/region, then scores with a pinned hap.py image and fixed truth files. Threading/memory are stripped from the miner config submissions and they will not cause divergence in the score. You can verify this by running a validator machine in demo mode and use GIAB truth set on a random GIAB sample to validate.  you will see scores will always stay deterministic.

Hey @bgCaτ [τ , ⛏] , HelixForge creates a new round-specific challenge region for each round. The number and distribution of injected variants vary by round because each round is generated independently and represents a different genomic context and disease.

I think I see why you’re asking this question. But the overcall penalty is not simply based on “how many synthetic mutations were injected.” The injected mutations are part of the hidden truth used to measure whether your configurations for the caller finds the right variants, but overcalling in this context is about extra variant calls that are not supported by the evaluation for that round.

So if as a miner, you call too many variants, you may recover more true sites, but you are also accumulating more false positives regardless of how many truth sites you are supposed to recover. That is what the overcall penalty is meant to discourage and this is why each round is different from the previous round. We are trying to discourage overfitting to these knobs by making the miners find generalizable patterns that always work. 

A better way to think about it is: the goal is not to estimate the number of injected mutations and tune around that number. You goal is to recover true variants while minimizing your false positives, similar to any real variant-calling analysis. But with the caveat that you will never get your false positive to 0.

What you’re describing is blinded evaluation in genomics.
In genomics, we do not have ground-truth VCFs for every sample. The field has advanced in the last decade using a very small number of deeply characterized truth genomes (GIAB cohort) which everyone has access to and uses to evaluate and improve methods on samples where ground truth information does not exist. So keeping the truth set held out is not an arbitrary decision, it is literally how the process works and these are the same constraint the field has had to work around for years.

Over the past 50 rounds, we’ve had 24 distinct winners. You can group winners by coldkey and inspect the distribution directly through the Minos MCP or API server. With the current ~87% burn rate, self-mining would be a lot of work for very little rational upside.

tl;dr the issue with your submission is that you defined   `--sample-ploidy 7` in your GATK config.  You should almost always select `2` for this flag unless you have very good reason not to. 

Longer answer: 
your config with `--sample-ploidy 7` failed because the sample is scored as a diploid human genome, meaning that we each have two copies of a chromosome, but the config  you submitted told GATK to call variants with the assumption that there are seven copies of each chromosome. That produced 7-copy genotypes like 0/0/0/0/1/1/1, which hap.py could not score against the diploid truth set, so the query effectively counted as empty. Barring very rare circumstances (like Down Syndrome where you have 3 copies of chromosome 21), humans almost always have two copies of each chromosome.

The setup we’re building is closer to a live scientific benchmark than a normal supervised-learning task that’s more common on training subnets here. 
107 miners are not supposed to train directly on the answer key as that defeats the whole purpose of this scientific endeavor, they’re supposed to submit better generalizable variant-calling strategies under uncertainty and improve them over time. Variant calls are not clean labels like AI training. they depend on alignment quality, repetitive regions, coverage, caller assumptions, filtering choices, etc and a lot of edge cases also exist where even strong pipelines disagree. 

Historically, the field has relied on a very small number of deeply characterized GIAB samples to develop callers. We want to expand that evaluation surface here. So the ground truth VCFs have to stay inside the validation layer only, at least while we establish the baseline across all 22 chromosomes. Otherwise miners are no longer improving general calling behavior and they are optimizing against the answer key. 
This is not also something that is hypothetical, it is how variant calling validation and improvement has been carried out for the past decade in genomics.

No, if HelixForge variants were different from real variants, that would defeat the whole purpose of what we’re building. The reason we have miners that are competitive and winning /rank in top 20, is precisely because they can use GIAB to tune their work. 

Fwiw we are finalizing the HelixForge research paper now so it can be peer reviewed by other experts in the field and we also will release examples of previously mapped chromosomes like chr20 in demo mode soon. 

The tl;dr of our previous conversations on this topic: variant calling has historically relied on a very small number of truth sets, especially these 7 GIAB samples. There is no truth file for every new genome, and most callers have been developed, tuned, and benchmarked around those same 7 GIAB samples. For example if you sequence your own genome, there is no truth file for it. But the variant caller that identifies your mutations was developed and tuned using GIAB. 
The right approach is to do what the field has been doing by iterating over GIAB/public truth sets, understand your false positives and false negatives, tune your config, and then generalize that performance to the unseen synthetic samples in the live round.

Answered in the DM ser. also sharing my repsonse here for others in case this comes up again:


Barring very specific edge cases that I don't even know of, you should almost always set `sample_ploidy` to `2` in your config.

Humans are diploid organisms, meaning we have two copies of each chromosome, so ploidy for human variant calling should basically be `2`. The only time it is 3 is on chr21 with Down Syndrome.

In this particular case, @DroneMe had `sample_ploidy=4`, which tells the caller the sample is tetraploid. That’s blueberry genome territory, not human genome. So the config failed scoring because it was built on a completely wrong biological assumption.

We would love to get more miners onboarded but we are currently constrained by run time. 

We initially had 64 UIDs and just bumped it to 80 last week. The reason it’s not higher is that our validation loop is intentionally strict. We’re using a NIST style benchmarking framework, which is basically the gold standard for evaluating variant calls, so we can’t cut corners without weakening the integrity of the whole system. Given that we have 72min rounds, that limits us on how many miners we can validate. 

Come to Novelty Search in a few hrs as I'll go into more detail about this, but the tl;dr is we treat each validator like an independent judge/reviewer of your work. They don’t just accept your VCF at face value, they need to reproduce it on their end, score it independently, and validate the result. And we need at least 2 validators to agree before we consider your work validated. So yeah, we’re limited for now, but we’re actively working on making this faster and scaling it up.
